Free Will Description: Individuals are unaware of the concept of free will. Their actions are purely reactive and instinctual, driven by basic survival needs. This is the bottom of Maslow's Pyramid, the Root Chakra, the first stage Piaget's cognitive development or step one of Erikson's psychosocial development etc.
Fiction Book: The Name of the Wind by Patrick Rothfuss
Practice Description: Grounding technique to manage anxiety and cravings by focusing on sensory inputs.
I: Journal: What kind of hero did I need to feel secure when I was a kid, and what would it take to become that person in the next 9 months?
IT: Make a journal for the APTITUDE project (or utilize the spreadsheet template and discord bot on offer), and develop the habit of recording your progress, tracking your Practices and Habits.
ITS: Journal: Make a list of systemic, social, and cultural influences that contribute to your sense of security
WE: Share, listen and react to the previous three exercises with your Digital Sangha

Free Will Description: Individual personalities (collections of habits—which are frequently repeated behaviors) are the combined effort of archetypal role models, including everything from fictional characters to societal celebrities (and perhaps ancient gods in polytheistic cultures)
Book: I am the Word by Paul Selig
Practice Description: Engaging in energy work to quiet the mind and promote a sense of connection.
I: Experience: Identify the "somatic address" of gratitude when you complete the IT exercise.
IT: Journal: Every day for this period, make a list of ten things you're grateful for, or 3 big things
ITS: Journal: engage in a 15 minute free write exploring your relationship with abundance vs scarcity (whatever that may be)
WE: Discuss abundance, scarcity and the law of reciprocity that the universe may or may not seem to adhere to from your perspective.

Free Will Description: Behavior is driven by a subconscious urge to alleviate the pain of emotions (that may or may not even be consciously noticed) that tell us that we are not enough. Without self-love toward these aversion based feelings, the tendency is to forge dominator "power over others."
Book: All About Love by bell hooks
Practice Description: Practicing feeling attuned to the power at the solar plexus and returning to it when doubt arises.
I: Journal: In a 15 minute free write, reflect on a time that the desire to dominate a person, situation, or yourself arose from shame that indicated a lack of self-love.
IT: Journal: make a list of 15 outside influences that tell you you're not enough. Once you're done, tear it up and throw it over your shoulder.
ITS: Journal: in a 15 minute free write, pick a social system you feel an attachment to that exercises power over others (healthcare, education, military, police, the family, your digital sangha, etc) and explore how it succeeds and fails at embodying self-love, and what it might look like with more.
WE: Discuss your ideas for bringing more self-love into the systems you wrote about, and/or to yourselves.

Free Will Description: Behavior is determined by the attempt to meet the expectations of the relationships that the individual is embedded within; we are defined by roles: partners, parents, children, coworkers, friends, pupils, etc
Fiction Book: Brave New World by Aldous Huxley
Practice Description: Fostering a state of loving-kindness and compassion to promote emotional stability.
I: Journal: Make a new column in your IT exercise. Write "I choose to do x because…" for each item
IT: Journal: Make the longest list you can of things you are forced to do for whatever reason. Begin each entry "I have to…"
ITS: Journal: make a list of 10 systems that demand your conformity. This can help inspire your answers to the IT exercise.
WE: Discuss how even within the demands of an unjust, corrupt, degrading society, we are still ultimately free to choose to participate.

Free Will Description: Behavior is based on chasing things valued by the culture: money, wealth, status, privilege, fame… in short, achievement. Although the question may arise, Free Will is still uninteresting and left largely unconsidered
Book: The Art of the Impossible by Steven Kotler
Practice Description: Inducing physiological changes to promote focus and emotional resilience.
I: Journal: Combine multiple Curiosities in service of tackling one Problem, and identify the Aspirational Identities that would belong to the person who travels that path.
IT: Journal: Make a list of 25 curiosities
ITS: Journal: Make a list of 15 problems that keep you up at night
WE: Share: Formulate your answer to the I exercise as a four quadrant vow and publicly sign your name to it in front of your supportive Digital Sangha

Free Will Description: Behavior is driven by a desire to be virtuous, to apply rules fairly, to reduce the influence of hierarchy, and to respect everyone's perspectives. Free Will is still absent as behavior follows predictably from a set of pluralistic heuristics.
Book: Luminous Darkness by Deborah Eden Tull AND/OR Nonviolent Communication by Marshall Rosenberg
Fiction Book: Wizard of Earthsea by Ursula K LeGuin
Practice Description: Integrating past experiences and emotions to enhance self-understanding and flow states.
On-Going Habit: Active Recovery (eg Yoga, Saunas, Ice Baths)
I: Journal: Write about a memory associated with each Shadow element from the IT exercise. Fully experience the event. Alchemically reframe each element as Light.
IT: Journal: Make a list of behaviors, characteristics and identities that comprise your Shadow
ITS: Journal: Return to your list of Problems from the previous level and identify the shadow characteristics that project them from the holonic level you occupy into the broader systems that cause them. This can help inspire your answers to the IT and I exercises.
WE: Share and discuss a few of the relationships between Shadow characteristics and Problems you've identified

Free Will Description: The ability to reflect on all the influences of stages prior to Yellow develops and the individual becomes convinced that Free Will is essentially an illusion.
Book: The Mind Illuminated by Culadasa (John Yates)
Practice Description: Integrating various practices to enhance systemic thinking and adaptability.
I: During meditation and group meet-ups, pay attention to what arises in your body without judgement, clinging, or aversion.
IT: Log: Begin logging meditation experiences with a focus on the Seven Factors of Enlightenment
ITS: Find (or start) an in-person meet up for meditation and/or dharma and attend at least once a week.
WE: Discuss how it feels to be a part of an in-person meet-up with your Digtal Sangha. Are you experiencing any challenges or successes?

Free Will Description: Spiritual development that leads to an experience of the nondual nature of the Kosmos that allows the Adept to burn off karma, develop nonreactivity, break unhealthy and ineffective patterns, escape samsara and unbridle from determinism effectively.
Fiction Book: Wise Man's Fear by Patrick Rothfuss
Practice Description: Encouraging spontaneous insight and innovation through creative problem-solving.
IT: At least once a week: Recall a behavior from the last 24 hours and rate your mindfulness as you engaged in it. Consider how you might have behaved differently with more mindfulness

Free Will Description: The obsession with "Free" Will of the previous two stages grows less all consuming and the goal becomes to subsume individual Will into alignment with the Will of Source. Blissful Union of Atman and Brahman.
Book: Finding Radical Wholeness by Ken Wilber
Practice Description: Developing deep concentration and mental stability through meditative absorption.

Free Will Description: There is no longer an individual who can have Free Will. This body becomes a perfect instrument of the evolution of consciousness, an empty Nobody who simply does the perfect thing in every situation—the thing that will result in the most spiritual growth for all involved.
Fiction Book: Autobiography of a Yogi by Yogananda
Practice Description: Enhancing insight into the nature of reality to promote non-dual awareness.
